name: Bolivia

colors.symbolism: the blood of those who died for the country and the bravery of the soldiers
the fertility of the land and Bolivia's natural wealth

description: Three equal horizontal stripes — red on top, yellow in the middle, green on the bottom — with the national coat of arms centred on the yellow band. The civil flag omits the coat of arms.
history: The red-yellow-green tricolour was adopted by President Manuel Isidoro Belzu on 5 November 1851 by Supreme Decree, replacing earlier red-green-red and yellow-green-red configurations. The current proportions and the design of the coat of arms have been adjusted several times; the modern arms with nine stars (one per department) date to 1888 with later refinements. Since 2009, the Wiphala — the chequered rainbow flag of the Andean indigenous peoples — has had equal status as a co-national flag of Bolivia.

features.symbolism: red for the blood spilt by Bolivia's heroes for the birth and preservation of the Republic, yellow for the nation's mineral wealth and green for the fertility of its land

features.role: national coat of arms (state flag only)
features.symbolism: an oval bearing Mount Potosí, an alpaca, a wheat sheaf and a breadfruit tree; surrounded by nine gold stars (one for each department) and the name BOLIVIA above; flanked by Bolivian flags, cannons, muskets, an axe and a phrygian cap of liberty, surmounted by an Andean condor with a laurel-and-olive wreath. The coat of arms is present only on the state flag (variant 'state' / 'state-ensign'); the civil flag (variant 'civil' / 'civil-ensign') is the plain tricolour
